verses.text: L'heure du jugement de Dieu est fixée ( **Ec 3.17 ** ; **8.6 ** ; **Dn 2.21 ** ; **Luc 21.22-24** ), mais nous ne la connaissons pas ( **Ac 1.7** ). Elle sera révélée au moment opportun ( **1Th 5.1-28 ** ; **1 Tim 6.15** ).
La loi, la littérature de sagesse et la prophétie ont tous condamné le déplacement des bornes ( **Dt 19.14 ** ; **27.17 ** ; **Pr 22.28 ** ; **23.10** ; voir note d'étude sur Os 5.10 ).
La loi n'autorisait pas la saisie des biens nécessaires en garantie pour un prêt ( **22.6-9 ** ; **Dt 24.6** , **10-13** , **17-21** ).

24:4 Literally, they push the poor out of their road in meeting them. Figuratively, they take advantage of them by force and injustice (alluding to the charge of Eliphaz, Job 22:8; 1 Sam 8:3). poor—in spirit and in circumstances (Matt 5:3). hide—from the injustice of their oppressors, who have robbed them of their all and driven them into unfrequented places (Job 20:19; Job 30:3-6; Pro 28:28).

24:5 wild asses— (Job 11:12). So Ishmael is called a "wild ass-man"; Hebrew (Gen 16:12). These Bedouin robbers, with the unbridled wildness of the ass of the desert, go forth thither. Robbery is their lawless "work." The desert, which yields no food to other men, yields food for the robber and his children by the plunder of caravans. rising betimes—In the East travelling is begun very early, before the heat comes on.

Job décrivait la condition désespérée des pauvres. Si leurs champs avaient été volés comme la vigne de Naboth ( **24.2 ** ; voir **1R 21.1-29** ), les pauvres récolteraient pour les propriétaires voleurs ce qui aurait dû être leurs propres récoltes ; au lieu de posséder toute la récolte, ils n'obtiendraient que les restes pour eux-mêmes. L'idée est peut-être que les pauvres étaient obligés de glaner dans les champs des méchants qui ne laissaient rien à glaner ( **Lv 19.10 ** ; **23.22** ), au lieu de pouvoir glaner auprès des justes, qui laissaient quelque chose pour les pauvres ( **Lv 19.9-10 ** ; **23.22 ** ; **Rt 2.1-23** ).
Les prêteurs injustes laissaient parfois les débiteurs sans vêtements ni couvertures, qu'ils avaient saisis en garantie des emprunts ( **22.6 ** ; **Ex 22.26-27 ** ; **Dt 24.11-13 ** ; **Am 2.8** ).

24:8 They are wet with the showers of the mountains,.... They that are without any clothes to cover them, lying down at the bottom of a hill or mountain, where the clouds often gather, and there break, or the snow at the top of them melts through the heat of the day; and whether by the one or by the other, large streams of water run down the mountains, and the naked poor, or such who are thinly clothed, are all over wet therewith, as Nebuchadnezzar's body was with the dew of heaven, when he was driven from men, and lived among beasts, Dan 4:33, and embrace the rock for want of a shelter;

24:10 (See on Job 22:6). In Job 24:7 a like sin is alluded to: but there he implies open robbery of garments in the desert; here, the more refined robbery in civilized life, under the name of a "pledge." Having stripped the poor, they make them besides labor in their harvest-fields and do not allow them to satisfy their hunger with any of the very corn which they carry to the heap. Worse treatment than that of the ox, according to Deu 25:4.

• Obliger les travailleurs à fouler le pressoir alors qu'ils souffrent de la soif viole le principe selon lequel les travailleurs doivent jouir d'une partie des produits qu'ils récoltent (voir **Dt 25.4 ** ; cf. **2Tm 2.6** ).

Pour Job, il semblait que contrairement au caractère même de Dieu ( **Ex 2.23-24 ** ; **Ps 12.5** ), Dieu ne soulageait pas la détresse des opprimés ( **Jb 24.2-12** ) et ne gênait pas les méchants dans leurs actions nuisibles envers leurs victimes ( **24.13-17** ).

24:13 So far as to openly committed sins; now, those done in the dark. Translate: "There are those among them (the wicked) who rebel," &c. light—both literal and figurative (John 3:19-20; Pro 2:13). paths thereof—places where the light shines.

24:14 with the light—at early dawn, while still dark, when the traveller in the East usually sets out, and the poor laborer to his work; the murderous robber lies in wait then (Ps 10:8). is as a thief—Thieves in the East steal while men sleep at night; robbers murder at early dawn. The same man who steals at night, when light dawns not only robs, but murders to escape detection.

24:15 The eye also of the adulterer waiteth for the twilight,.... Not of the morning, which would not give him time enough to satiate his lust, but of the evening, that he may have the whole night before him to gratify his impure desires, and that these may be indulged in the most private and secret manner; and having fixed the time in the evening with his adulteress, he waits with impatience, and earnestly wishes and longs for its coming, and diligently looks out for the close of day, and takes the first opportunity of the darkness of the evening to set out on his adventure, see Pro 7:7;

24:16 dig through—Houses in the East are generally built of sun-dried mud bricks (so Matt 6:19). "Thieves break through," literally, "dig through" (Ezek 12:7). had marked—Rather, as in Job 9:7, "They shut themselves up" (in their houses); literally, "they seal up." for themselves—for their own ends, namely, to escape detection. know not—shun.

24:17 They shrink from the "morning" light, as much as other men do from the blackest darkness ("the shadow of death"). if one know—that is, recognize them. Rather, "They know well (are familiar with) the terrors of," &c. [UMBREIT]. Or, as MAURER, "They know the terrors of (this) darkness," namely, of morning, the light, which is as terrible to them as darkness ("the shadow of death") is to other men.

Ces sentiments semblent s'opposer aux observations de Job, de sorte que certains érudits suggèrent qu'il s'agit des paroles de Bildad ou de Sofar. Cependant, Job a pu prononcer des malédictions sur les méchants, citer de manière sarcastique les opinions de ses amis ou dire que le sort des méchants présenté par Élifaz ( **5.2-7 ** ; **15.17-25** ), Bildad ( **8.8-19 ** ; **18.5-21** ), et Sofar ( **20.4-29** ) s'abat également sur les justes ( **21.23-26** ).\n\n

24:25 And if it be not so now,.... If this is not the case of men of such wicked lives as above described, do not prosper in the world, and increase in riches, and do not pass through the world with impunity, and die quietly, in the full possession of their honour and wealth: who will make me a liar? where is the man? let him stand forth and appear, and disprove what has been said, and make out the doctrine delivered to be false doctrine, and a lie;
